name: Sundara-Pandya
dates_notes: Pre-Śaṅkara; cited by Sureśvara in the Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi; verses preserved as quotations only.
dating_methodology: Sundara-Pāṇḍya is dated by citation-evidence in Sureśvara and (with much greater uncertainty) in later Śrīvaiṣṇava polemical literature. Terminus ante quem: Sureśvara's *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* (later eighth century) cites verses attributed to Sundara-Pāṇḍya as authoritative against the *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* opponent. He must therefore antedate the late eighth century; if the verses circulated as authoritative by Sureśvara's time, they probably originated at least a generation earlier. Terminus post quem: the verses presuppose the developed Vedānta-Mīmāṃsā controversy over *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*, which is mature only by the mid-seventh century. The window 600–700 CE follows Hiriyanna (*Outlines*, 1932) and Sastri's introduction to the *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* (1925); it places Sundara-Pāṇḍya in the immediately pre-Śaṅkara generation. Vedānta Deśika's later identification of a Sundara-Pāṇḍya as a Pāñcarātra-aligned Viśiṣṭādvaita ancestor (in the *Pāñcarātra-Rakṣā*) probably refers to a different figure of the same regional name and is rejected by most modern scholarship as confusion. Note (AI-generated audit): the dating is essentially circular — he is placed in the seventh century *because* he is the kind of pre-Śaṅkara figure Sureśvara cites with approval, and Sureśvara is dated by reference to Śaṅkara. No external attestation, no manuscript, no autograph; the regional name 'Pāṇḍya' suggests Tamil-region provenance but does not by itself date the figure.
core_thesis:
Sundara-Pāṇḍya, on the verses cited under his name in Sureśvara's *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* book 1, holds three coupled commitments that are recognizable as proto-Advaita without being identical to the developed Śaṅkara-school doctrine. The first is the essential non-difference of self and Brahman as the settled meaning of the upaniṣadic identity-statements (*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.66, the verse-cluster Sureśvara introduces with explicit attribution). The second is the structural unfitness of *karma* — bound as it is to action, agency, and the falsely-attributed *kartṛtva* — to be a means to a liberation that consists in the cessation of all such attribution (*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.67–68). The third is the sufficiency of correct upaniṣadic apprehension to terminate ignorance (*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.69, on Sureśvara's framing). The three theses are dialectically interlocked. If liberation consists in the cessation of false agency-attribution, then *karma*, which presupposes and reinforces *kartṛtva*, cannot produce it; only the cognitive recognition of the non-different self, which dissolves *kartṛtva*-attribution, can (the argument-form Sureśvara extracts at *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.51–69). The position is therefore a structural argument against *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*: not the empirical claim that ritual is unnecessary, but the categorial claim that ritual is the wrong kind of cause for the kind of cessation liberation requires (*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.52).

The argument is sharper than the bare statement of immediacy-of-knowledge that Śaṅkara (*Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.1.4) and Sureśvara (*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.1–5) will systematize. Sundara-Pāṇḍya, on the verses Sureśvara preserves, is not arguing that ritual is harmful or to be abandoned; the position is consistent with continued ritual observance as preparatory or as part of the social-religious frame within which upaniṣadic study takes place (consistent with the framing at *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.5). What he denies is that *karma* contributes to the production of liberating cognition (*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.67). The cognition is not produced; it is recognized. The denial is structural, not behavioral. This distinction matters because it is exactly what Brahmadatta's *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* contests: Brahmadatta, on the *pūrvapakṣa* reconstruction at *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.51 and (parallel) Maṇḍana's *Brahma-Siddhi* preamble, holds that liberating cognition is itself the kind of cognitive event whose generation requires *bhāvanā*-cultivation, and *bhāvanā*-cultivation is a species of enjoined activity. Sundara-Pāṇḍya's verses, on Sureśvara's reading (*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.66–69), refuse this assimilation: the recognition of non-difference is not produced by activity, because it is the cessation of the activity-frame as such.

The philosophical force of the position is dialectical. Sundara-Pāṇḍya supplies Sureśvara with a pre-Śaṅkara authority for the immediacy-of-knowledge thesis, against the *bhāvanā*-school of Brahmadatta and Maṇḍana (the citation-cluster at *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.66–69 functioning as *āptavākya* in the argument). The fact that Sureśvara needs such an authority is itself evidence that the immediacy-thesis was contested in the immediately pre-Śaṅkara generation (Hiriyanna, *Outlines of Indian Philosophy*, 1932, ch. on the *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi*). Sundara-Pāṇḍya's verses, as preserved at *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.66–69, do not articulate the developed *māyā*-doctrine (Śaṅkara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 2.1.14), the *adhyāsa-bhāṣya* analysis of superimposition (Śaṅkara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* preamble), or the doctrine of *sākṣi-caitanya* that distinguishes mature Advaita (Sureśvara, *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 2.86ff.); they articulate the more basic non-difference / immediacy / *karma*-inertness cluster. He is therefore proto-Advaitin in the precise sense that the structural commitments of the position are present without the developed metaphysics. Whether he was, in his own self-understanding, a member of an identifiable school is not preserved; whether he was Tamil-region, as his name suggests, and whether he is to be identified with any other named figure of the same region, are unsettled questions that the citation-evidence cannot resolve (Hiriyanna, *Outlines*, 1932; Mahadevan, *The Sambandha-Vārttika*, 1958, introduction).

What the citation does not preserve is Sundara-Pāṇḍya's positive account of how the recognition of non-difference operates as a cognitive event. Sureśvara's own answer — direct upaniṣadic *anubhava* generated by the *mahāvākya* (*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 2.81–95) — may or may not be Sundara-Pāṇḍya's; the verses preserved at *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.66–69 give the negative theses (against *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*, against *karma* as cause of liberation) more sharply than the positive epistemology. The conservative claim is that Sundara-Pāṇḍya held the structural commitments that distinguish the immediacy-school from the *bhāvanā*-school, and that Sureśvara's appeal to him is philologically warranted by the existence of an actual textual source whose contents we recover only through the citations (Hiriyanna, *Outlines*, 1932; Sastri, ed., *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi*, 1925, introduction).

engaged_works.title: Lost verses cited in Naishkarmya-Siddhi
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Verse-corpus is lost; preserved only as citations within Sureśvara's Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi.
engaged_works.summary: Sundara-Pāṇḍya's literary corpus is lost. What survives is a set of verses (the most-cited cluster appears at *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.66–69, where Sureśvara invokes them as authoritative against the *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* opponent) which Sureśvara introduces with explicit attribution and treats as authoritative on the side of immediate *jñāna* as the direct cause of *mokṣa*. The verses articulate three coupled theses. First, the essential non-difference of self and Brahman as the settled meaning of the upaniṣadic identity-statements (*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.66). Second, the structural unfitness of *karma* to be a means to a liberation consisting in the cessation of agency-attribution: *karma* presupposes the *kartṛtva* whose cancellation is the very content of liberation, so *karma* cannot produce liberation (*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.67–68). Third, the sufficiency of correct upaniṣadic apprehension to terminate ignorance, with the negative implication that no quantity of additional ritual cultivation contributes to that termination (*Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.69). The position is recognizably proto-Advaita: the structural commitments of the immediacy-school are present without the developed *māyā* (Śaṅkara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 2.1.14), *adhyāsa* (Śaṅkara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* preamble), or *sākṣi-caitanya* (Sureśvara, *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 2.86ff.) doctrines that mature Advaita systematizes. Sureśvara's appeal to Sundara-Pāṇḍya at *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi* 1.66–69 is the principal evidence that the immediacy-thesis was contested in the immediately pre-Śaṅkara generation and that Sureśvara needed a pre-Śaṅkara authority to ground the position against the *bhāvanā*-school of Brahmadatta and Maṇḍana (cf. Maṇḍana's *Brahma-Siddhi* preamble). Vedānta Deśika's later mention of a Sundara-Pāṇḍya in the *Pāñcarātra-Rakṣā* probably refers to a different figure of the same regional name and is generally rejected as the same author by modern scholarship (Hiriyanna, *Outlines*, 1932; Sastri, ed., *Naiṣkarmya-Siddhi*, 1925, introduction). Nothing of his original work, its title, or its full extent can be recovered.
